# Web Security — Complete Senior Frontend / Full-Stack Interview Deep Dive

Web security is one of the most important topics for **Senior Frontend / Full-Stack / Architect** interviews.

Interviewers ask this because it tests:

✅ Deep understanding of the browser threat model

✅ Real-world experience with attacks

✅ Ability to design secure systems

✅ Enterprise-grade authentication & authorization

✅ Handling denial-of-service attacks

✅ Modern browser protections (CSP, SameSite, HSTS)

✅ Prevention of vulnerabilities

Used in production apps like:

```txt
Amazon
Facebook
Google
Vercel
Slack
LinkedIn
Netflix
Uber
Airbnb
Notion
```

Below is a **complete detailed explanation** for all 14 topics you provided.

Everything you need to pass any Senior React / Frontend security round confidently.

---

# 1. Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)

## What is XSS?

XSS lets attackers inject malicious JavaScript into web pages.

If the browser trusts the injected script, the attacker can:

- Steal cookies
- Hijack sessions
- Redirect users
- Modify UI
- Deface websites

XSS is the **#1 web vulnerability**.

---

## Types of XSS

### 1. Reflected XSS

Attacker sends a URL like:

```txt
example.com/?q=<script>alert(1)</script>
```

Server reflects the input into HTML without escaping.

### 2. Stored XSS

Attacker stores malicious script in database.

Every user sees it (blog comments, chat).

### 3. DOM-based XSS

Client-side code writes user input to DOM unsafely:

```js
document.body.innerHTML = window.location.hash;
```

---

## Real-World Example

Twitter suffered from XSS worms that spread across accounts.

Any site using `innerHTML` unsafely is vulnerable.

---

## Prevention

✅ Escape all output

✅ Use `textContent` instead of `innerHTML`

✅ Use frameworks (React auto-escapes)

✅ Sanitize user input (DOMPurify)

✅ Implement CSP

✅ Encode user data

✅ Use `X-XSS-Protection` (limited)

---

## Example — React Auto Escapes

```jsx
<p>{userInput}</p>
```

React escapes HTML by default.

But this is dangerous:

```jsx
<div
  dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
    __html: userInput,
  }}
/>
```

Only use if input is sanitized.

---

# 2. Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)

## What is CSRF?

CSRF tricks a logged-in user into performing unwanted actions.

Example:

- User logs into bank
- Visits malicious site
- Site auto-submits form:

https://bank.com/transfer&nbsp; &lt;input name="to" value="attacker" /&gt;&nbsp; &lt;input name="amount" value="10000" /&gt;&lt;/form&gt;![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Bank cookies are sent automatically.

Money gets transferred.

---

## Prevention

✅ Use SameSite cookies (`Lax`/`Strict`)

✅ Use CSRF tokens

✅ Use custom headers (X-CSRF-Token)

✅ Double-submit cookie pattern

✅ Origin/Referer validation

✅ Avoid GET for state-changing operations

---

## Example — CSRF Token

Backend sends token → Frontend adds it to request:

```js
fetch("/api/transfer", {
  method: "POST",
  headers: {
    "X-CSRF-Token": token
  },
  body: JSON.stringify({...})
});
```

Server verifies token before processing.

---

# 3. XML External Entity (XXE)

## What is XXE?

Attack on XML parsers.

Attackers inject malicious XML like:

```xml
<!DOCTYPE data [
<!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>

<data>&xxe;</data>
```

The parser fetches files or triggers requests.

---

## Real-World Example

- SoundCloud once had XXE letting attackers read internal server files.
- WordPress had a similar issue.

---

## Prevention

✅ Disable external entity loading

✅ Use safe XML parsers

✅ Prefer JSON over XML

✅ Avoid untrusted XML input

Modern frontends rarely use XML — but SOAP APIs are still vulnerable.

---

# 4. Authentication + Best Practices

## What is Authentication?

Authentication verifies **who the user is**.

Examples:

- Password
- OTP
- Biometric
- Passkey
- OAuth

---

## Best Practices

✅ Hash passwords with bcrypt/argon2

✅ Use HTTPS everywhere

✅ Use secure session tokens

✅ Enforce multi-factor authentication (MFA)

✅ Use OAuth 2 / OIDC

✅ Rotate refresh tokens

✅ Avoid storing JWT in localStorage

✅ Use HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite cookies

✅ Enforce password strength

✅ Rate-limit login attempts

✅ Detect account takeover attempts

---

## Modern Authentication Stack

- OAuth 2.0
- OpenID Connect
- WebAuthn / Passkeys
- Refresh tokens
- HttpOnly cookies
- Silent refresh with interceptor

Used in enterprise apps like:

- Vercel
- Slack
- LinkedIn
- Notion

---

# 5. Authorization + Best Practices

## What is Authorization?

Authorization determines **what user can do**.

Examples:

- Admin → full access
- Manager → team access
- Viewer → read-only

---

## Types

### 1. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

Based on user roles.

### 2. Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

Based on attributes (location, device, time).

### 3. Policy-Based Access Control (PBAC)

Rules and policies.

---

## Best Practices

✅ Never trust client-side authorization

✅ Enforce checks on backend

✅ Use minimum privilege

✅ Use JWT scopes

✅ Log access attempts

✅ Prevent Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR)

✅ Use tools like Auth0 or Okta

---

## Example IDOR Vulnerability

Bad:

```txt
GET /orders/123
```

Anyone can guess IDs and access others' orders.

Fix:

- Check ownership
- Use UUIDs
- Return 404 for unauthorized attempts

Used in enterprise dashboards.

---

# 6. Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS)

## What is DDoS?

Flooding servers with traffic → service goes down.

DDoS = Distributed DoS.

---

## Common Types

- Volumetric attacks
- Protocol attacks
- Application-layer attacks

---

## Real-World Cases

- GitHub 2018 DDoS (1.35 Tbps)
- Google 2020 (2.5 Tbps)
- Dyn 2016 attack shut down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit

---

## Frontend Prevention

✅ Use CDN

✅ Enable rate limiting

✅ Use WAF (Web Application Firewall)

✅ Use bot protection

✅ Enable request throttling

✅ Cache aggressively

✅ Enable HTTP/2 and HTTP/3

---

## Backend/Infra Prevention

- AWS Shield
- Cloudflare
- Fastly
- Akamai
- Anycast networks

---

# 7. CORS Attack

## What is CORS?

CORS = Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

Governs how one domain can interact with another.

Browsers block unsafe cross-origin requests by default.

---

## What Attackers Try

- Steal API responses
- Perform actions using user credentials
- Access private data

---

## Correct CORS Configuration

Never do:

```txt
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
```

For sensitive APIs.

Instead:

- Whitelist specific origins
- Deny unauthorized requests
- Reject wildcard for credentials

---

## Best Practices

✅ Enforce strict origin lists

✅ Validate credentials

✅ Use CSRF tokens

✅ Avoid overly permissive headers

---

# 8. Cross-Frame Scripting (XFS)

Attack embeds vulnerable site inside iframe:

https://bank.com![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

Uses:

- Clickjacking
- UI redressing
- Frame injection

---

## Real-World Example

- Facebook once had clickjacking attacks that tricked users into liking pages.

---

## Prevention

✅ Use `X-Frame-Options: DENY`

✅ Use CSP `frame-ancestors`

✅ Detect frame breakout

Example:

```txt
Content-Security-Policy:
frame-ancestors 'none';
```

---

# 9. Keeping the Web Safe and Secure

Modern security is layered:

### 1. Application Layer

- Escape user input
- Validate data
- Sanitize HTML

### 2. Network Layer

- HTTPS
- HSTS
- Secure DNS

### 3. Authentication Layer

- OAuth
- OIDC
- Passkeys

### 4. Infrastructure Layer

- WAF
- Rate limiting
- DDoS shield
- Bot detection

### 5. Monitoring Layer

- Sentry
- Datadog
- Splunk
- SIEM

---

## Enterprise Security Checklist

✅ HTTPS with HSTS

✅ CSP

✅ SameSite cookies

✅ Input validation

✅ Secure authentication

✅ Rate limiting

✅ Monitoring + alerts

✅ Regular penetration tests

✅ Dependency scanning

✅ CI/CD security checks

---

# 10. CSP Directives + Secure Implementation

## What is CSP?

Content Security Policy prevents:

- XSS
- Clickjacking
- Data injection

Example:

```txt
Content-Security-Policy:
default-src 'self';
script-src 'self' https://cdn.com;
style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';
img-src 'self' data:;
```

Restricts what can load.

---

## Common Directives

- `default-src`
- `script-src`
- `style-src`
- `img-src`
- `font-src`
- `connect-src`
- `frame-ancestors`

---

## Best Practices

✅ Avoid `unsafe-inline`

✅ Use nonces or hashes

✅ Whitelist minimal sources

✅ Use strict CSP

✅ Enforce via headers

---

## Real-World Example

Vercel, GitHub, Notion, Slack enforce strict CSP.

Reduces XSS attacks by 90%+.

---

# 11. Prototype Pollution Attacks

Attack modifies JavaScript prototypes:

```js
Object.prototype.polluted = true;

const obj = {};

console.log(obj.polluted); // true
```

Can cause:

- Server-side bugs
- Auth bypass
- App crashes
- Injection into all objects

---

## Vulnerable Libraries

- Lodash < 4.17.21
- jQuery
- Older utility libs

---

## Prevention

✅ Freeze prototypes:

```js
Object.freeze(Object.prototype);
```

✅ Use safe object literals

✅ Validate keys

✅ Avoid recursive merges

Modern libraries (Lodash-es, es-toolkit) fix this.

---

# 12. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

SSRF tricks the server into making internal requests:

```txt
GET /fetch?url=http://internal-service
```

Attackers use it to:

- Read AWS metadata
- Access private DBs
- Attack internal APIs

---

## Real-World Example

Capital One breach in 2019 used SSRF to steal 100M records.

---

## Prevention

✅ Whitelist allowed URLs

✅ Block internal IP ranges

✅ Use metadata firewall

✅ Validate URLs strictly

✅ Use IMDSv2 in AWS

Modern serverless platforms (Vercel, AWS Lambda) mitigate SSRF risks.

---

# 13. Session Management + Best Practices

Sessions are essential for user identification.

---

## Best Practices

✅ Use secure session cookies

✅ SameSite=Lax or Strict

✅ HttpOnly cookies

✅ Secure flag

✅ Rotate session IDs

✅ Enforce session expiration

✅ Detect concurrent sessions

✅ Use refresh token rotation

✅ Kill session on password change

✅ Log all session activity

---

## Modern Session Strategies

- Short-lived JWT + refresh token
- Session ID stored in HttpOnly cookies
- Auth server issues new session on refresh

Used by:

- Google
- Facebook
- LinkedIn
- Slack

---

## Inactivity Session Timeout

Enterprise apps auto-logout after inactivity.

Great for banking apps.

Example:

```js
let timer;

document.addEventListener("mousemove", () => {
  clearTimeout(timer);

  timer = setTimeout(logout, 15 * 60 * 1000);
});
```

---

# 14. Code Injection

Code injection attacks include:

- SQL injection
- OS command injection
- JavaScript injection
- Template injection

Attacker executes arbitrary code on server or browser.

---

## Real Examples

- SQL Injection compromised Sony Pictures
- Command injection compromised e-commerce sites
- Server-side template injection led to RCE at large SaaS platforms

---

## Prevention

✅ Never trust user input

✅ Escape queries (parameterized SQL)

✅ Avoid `eval`, `Function()`

✅ Sanitize HTML

✅ Use secure templating engines

✅ Validate input strictly

✅ Use ORMs (Prisma, TypeORM)

---

## Example — Bad SQL

```js
db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=${id}`);
```

Attackers inject:

```txt
1 OR 1=1
```

Fix:

```js
db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?", [id]);
```

Standard best practice.

---

# 15. Combining All Security Layers

Enterprise apps use a **multi-layer defense**:

```txt
Frontend Sanitization
    ↓
Escaping
    ↓
CSP
    ↓
Secure Cookies
    ↓
API Rate Limiting
    ↓
WAF
    ↓
Backend Validation
    ↓
Database Escape
    ↓
Monitoring
    ↓
Incident Response
```

Real-world example:

- Amazon
- Facebook
- Google
- Vercel
- Netflix

All use layered defense to protect billions of users.

---

# 16. Senior React / Frontend Interview Answer

> Web security is a layered, holistic engineering discipline. On the client side, we prevent XSS by using safe rendering, CSP, and input sanitization. CSRF is prevented using CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies. Authentication follows industry standards like OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect, WebAuthn, and passkeys, with refresh tokens rotated and stored in HttpOnly cookies. Authorization is always enforced server-side using RBAC, ABAC, or PBAC. Attacks like XXE, SSRF, prototype pollution, and code injection require strict input validation, safe parsers, and hardened dependencies. Enterprise applications defend against DDoS using CDNs, WAFs, and rate limiting, while browser attacks like clickjacking are mitigated using CSP frame ancestors and X-Frame-Options. Modern security requires multiple layers — network, application, authentication, monitoring — and continuous updates. This approach mirrors what large-scale systems used by Google, Amazon, Facebook, and Netflix implement to protect billions of users worldwide.

# Advanced Web Security Deep Dive

## Session Management Best Practices • CSP Directives + Examples • XSS Prevention Techniques

These are three of the most important **Senior Frontend / Full-Stack security interview topics**.

They test:

✅ Real-world engineering skills

✅ Deep browser security understanding

✅ Ability to design enterprise-grade authentication

✅ Understanding of modern secure headers

✅ Knowledge of frontend attack vectors

✅ Ability to protect apps at scale

Used in production systems at:

```txt
Amazon
Google
Facebook
Slack
Netflix
LinkedIn
Vercel
Uber
Airbnb
Notion
```

Let’s break each section in complete detail.

---

# 1. Session Management Best Practices

Session management is one of the **most attacked layers** of web apps.

Poor session security causes:

- Account hijacking
- Data leaks
- CSRF vulnerabilities
- Token theft
- Persistent unauthorized access

Modern apps must follow strict rules to avoid these issues.

---

## What is a Session?

A session is a **user's authenticated state** on a website.

The server or client stores identifiers such as:

- Session ID (cookie)
- Access token (JWT)
- Refresh token

Whenever the user makes a request, the server identifies them.

---

## Types of Session Storage

### 1. Server-side Sessions

- Server stores session data
- Client stores only session ID (in cookie)
- Great for high-security apps

### 2. Client-side Sessions

- JWT stored in browser
- Signed by server
- Great for scalability

### 3. Hybrid Sessions

- JWT + refresh token
- HttpOnly cookies + short-lived access token
- Enterprise pattern

---

## Best Practices

### ✅ 1. Use HttpOnly Cookies

Prevents JavaScript from accessing cookies:

```txt
Set-Cookie: session=xyz; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax
```

Blocks:

- XSS token theft
- Session hijacking

Used by:

- Google
- Facebook
- Vercel

---

### ✅ 2. Enable Secure Flag

Cookie sent only over HTTPS.

Prevents leakage over HTTP.

```txt
Set-Cookie: session=xyz; Secure;
```

---

### ✅ 3. Use SameSite Cookies

Prevents CSRF attacks:

- `Strict` = strongest
- `Lax` = safe default
- `None` = required for cross-origin only

```txt
SameSite=Lax
```

Most banking apps use `Strict`.

---

### ✅ 4. Rotate Session IDs

On:

- Login
- Logout
- Privilege change

Prevents:

- Session fixation attacks
- Reuse of leaked IDs

---

### ✅ 5. Use Short-Lived Access Tokens

Access tokens should expire in:

- 5–15 minutes

Reduces damage if leaked.

Refresh tokens issue new access tokens.

---

### ✅ 6. Use Refresh Token Rotation

Every refresh should:

- Issue new refresh token
- Revoke old one

Prevents:

- Token replay attacks
- Persistent hijacking

Used by:

- Vercel
- LinkedIn
- Slack

---

### ✅ 7. Bind Sessions to Device Fingerprint

Optional but strong:

- Device ID
- IP range
- Browser fingerprint

If mismatch → force logout.

Used by:

- Banking apps
- Coinbase
- PayPal

---

### ✅ 8. Enforce Session Expiration

Two types:

- Absolute expiration (24h max)
- Idle expiration (15–30 min inactivity)

Balance security + UX.

---

### ✅ 9. Multi-Device Session Management

Enterprise apps allow:

- View active sessions
- Log out from other devices
- Kill single session

Used by:

- Gmail
- WhatsApp
- LinkedIn

---

### ✅ 10. Automatic Logout on Suspicious Activity

Enterprise systems detect:

- New location
- New device
- Failed logins
- Unusual IP

Force logout and require MFA.

---

### ✅ 11. Prevent Session Hijacking

Techniques:

✅ HttpOnly cookies

✅ CSRF token

✅ Same-origin requests

✅ SSL pinning (mobile)

✅ Token rotation

✅ Detect anomalies

---

### ✅ 12. Log Session Activity

Enterprise systems log:

- Login attempts
- Refresh events
- IP changes
- User-agent changes

Used for:

- Threat detection
- Compliance
- Auditing

---

### ✅ 13. Use OAuth 2.1 + OIDC

Modern authentication uses:

- OAuth 2.1 (secure defaults)
- OIDC for identity
- PKCE for public clients
- Rotating refresh tokens

Used by:

- Google
- Auth0
- Okta
- Vercel

---

### ✅ 14. Use Passkeys / WebAuthn

Modern secure authentication:

- Public key cryptography
- Phishing resistant
- No shared secrets
- Better UX

Used by:

- Google
- Apple
- Microsoft
- Notion

---

### ✅ 15. Kill Session on Password Change

If user updates password:

- All existing sessions must be invalidated
- Force re-login

Important for:

- Preventing session takeover
- Compliance rules

---

# 2. CSP Directives — Complete Deep Dive + Examples

CSP (Content Security Policy) is the **most powerful browser-side protection** against:

✅ XSS

✅ Clickjacking

✅ Data injection

✅ Third-party abuse

✅ Malicious CDN attacks

Enterprises like GitHub, Slack, LinkedIn use CSP aggressively.

---

## What is CSP?

CSP is a response header telling the browser:

```txt
Only load these resources from these origins
```

Sample header:

```txt
Content-Security-Policy:
default-src 'self';
script-src 'self' https://cdn.example.com;
style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';
img-src 'self' data:;
connect-src 'self';
frame-ancestors 'none';
```

---

## Common CSP Directives

### 1. `default-src`

The **fallback** for all resource types.

```txt
default-src 'self';
```

Meaning:

- Everything must come from same domain unless overridden.

---

### 2. `script-src`

Controls scripts:

```txt
script-src 'self' https://cdn.example.com;
```

Blocks:

- Inline JS
- Untrusted scripts

Great for XSS prevention.

Enterprise best practice:

```txt
script-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123';
```

Only scripts with matching nonce are executed.

Used by:

- Vercel
- GitHub
- Slack

---

### 3. `style-src`

Controls CSS:

```txt
style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';
```

`'unsafe-inline'` is discouraged.

Better:

```txt
style-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123';
```

---

### 4. `img-src`

Controls images:

```txt
img-src 'self' data: https://cdn.example.com;
```

Allow:

- Data URIs
- Whitelisted CDN
- Same origin

---

### 5. `connect-src`

Controls fetch/XHR/WebSocket:

```txt
connect-src 'self' https://api.example.com wss://socket.example.com;
```

Blocks:

- Malicious API calls
- Data exfiltration

---

### 6. `font-src`

Controls fonts:

```txt
font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com;
```

Prevents:

- Rogue font hosts
- Font-based attacks

---

### 7. `frame-src`

Controls what can be embedded:

```txt
frame-src 'self' https://youtube.com;
```

---

### 8. `frame-ancestors`

Prevents clickjacking:

```txt
frame-ancestors 'none';
```

Only allows same-origin embedding.

Recommended for:

- Banking apps
- Dashboards
- Admin panels

---

### 9. `object-src`

Should almost always be:

```txt
object-src 'none';
```

Blocks Flash, plugins.

---

### 10. `base-uri`

Prevents attackers from changing `<base>` URL:

```txt
base-uri 'self';
```

---

### 11. `form-action`

Restricts form submissions:

```txt
form-action 'self';
```

Prevents:

- Cross-origin form redirection
- Phishing

---

### 12. `worker-src`

Restricts Web Workers:

```txt
worker-src 'self';
```

---

### 13. `manifest-src`

Restricts PWA manifest:

```txt
manifest-src 'self';
```

---

### 14. `upgrade-insecure-requests`

Automatically upgrade HTTP → HTTPS:

```txt
upgrade-insecure-requests;
```

Great for older sites.

---

### 15. `report-to` / `report-uri`

Send CSP violations to a server:

```txt
report-to /csp-endpoint;
```

Used for:

- Monitoring
- Alerts
- Improving policy

---

## Strong Enterprise CSP Example

```txt
Content-Security-Policy:
default-src 'self';
script-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123';
style-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123';
img-src 'self' data: https://cdn.example.com;
font-src 'self' https://fonts.gstatic.com;
connect-src 'self' https://api.example.com wss://socket.example.com;
frame-ancestors 'none';
object-src 'none';
form-action 'self';
base-uri 'self';
upgrade-insecure-requests;
report-to /csp-endpoint;
```

Reduces XSS attacks by \~90%.

Used at:

- LinkedIn
- Vercel
- Slack
- Notion

---

## CSP Best Practices

✅ Avoid `unsafe-inline`

✅ Avoid `unsafe-eval`

✅ Use nonces or hashes

✅ Strict `frame-ancestors 'none'`

✅ Restrict `object-src`

✅ Force HTTPS

✅ Whitelist minimal origins

✅ Monitor violations

Enterprises rely on CSP as one of the strongest defenses.

---

# 3. XSS Prevention Techniques — Complete Deep Dive

XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) is:

✅ The #1 web vulnerability

✅ Extremely dangerous

✅ Present in millions of apps

Attackers can:

- Steal cookies
- Hijack accounts
- Modify UI
- Insert malware
- Redirect users
- Deface content

Prevention requires a **multi-layer approach**.

---

## Types of XSS

### 1. Reflected XSS

Injected input reflected in URL.

Example:

```txt
example.com/?q=<script>alert(1)</script>
```

### 2. Stored XSS

Payload stored in DB.

Every visitor sees it.

Example:

- Blog comments
- Chat messages
- Reviews

### 3. DOM-based XSS

DOM writes untrusted data unsafely:

```js
document.body.innerHTML = window.location.hash;
```

---

## Prevention Techniques

### ✅ 1. Escape All Output

Never insert user data raw.

React automatically escapes:

```jsx
<div>{userInput}</div>
```

Safe.

---

### ✅ 2. Avoid `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`

Bad:

```jsx
<div
  dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
    __html: userInput,
  }}
/>
```

Only use with:

- DOMPurify sanitization

```js
import DOMPurify from "dompurify";

<div
  dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
    __html: DOMPurify.sanitize(userInput),
  }}
/>;
```

---

### ✅ 3. Use `textContent` Instead of `innerHTML`

```js
element.textContent = userInput;
```

Never:

```js
element.innerHTML = userInput;
```

---

### ✅ 4. Sanitize Rich Text Input

If your app accepts HTML (blog editor, comments):

Use:

- DOMPurify
- sanitize-html
- js-xss

Whitelisted tags only:

```js
DOMPurify.sanitize(input, {
  ALLOWED_TAGS: ["b", "i", "p"],
});
```

---

### ✅ 5. Use CSP

CSP blocks inline scripts:

```txt
script-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123';
```

Even if attacker injects `<script>`, it won't execute.

Used by all top security-focused enterprises.

---

### ✅ 6. Validate Input on Backend

Enforce:

- Length limits
- Format
- Whitelisted characters
- Type validation

Never rely on frontend validation only.

---

### ✅ 7. Escape Data When Rendering in URL

Bad:

```js
window.location = "/search?q=" + userInput;
```

Fix:

```js
window.location = "/search?q=" + encodeURIComponent(userInput);
```

---

### ✅ 8. Avoid `eval` and `Function()`

Bad:

```js
eval(userInput);
```

XSS vector.

---

### ✅ 9. Use Trusted Types (Chrome)

Trusted Types prevent DOM XSS:

```txt
Content-Security-Policy:
require-trusted-types-for 'script';
```

Chrome enforces safer DOM APIs.

Used by:

- Google
- Vercel
- Cloudflare

---

### ✅ 10. Use Framework Escaping (React / Vue / Angular)

Modern frameworks escape by default.

Never bypass:

- React → avoid `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`
- Vue → avoid `v-html`
- Angular → avoid `bypassSecurityTrustHtml`

---

### ✅ 11. Sanitize URLs

Bad:

```jsx
<a href={userLink}>Click</a>
```

Attackers may inject:

```txt
javascript:alert(1)
```

Fix:

```js
const safeUrl = userLink.startsWith("http") ? userLink : "#";
```

---

### ✅ 12. Add Security Headers

```txt
X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
X-Frame-Options: DENY
X-XSS-Protection: 0
Referrer-Policy: same-origin
Permissions-Policy: interest-cohort=()
```

Modern browsers rely more on CSP.

---

### ✅ 13. Prefer JSON APIs Over HTML APIs

JSON is safer than server-side HTML rendering.

React renders JSON as text.

Never inject HTML from server.

---

### ✅ 14. Use HTML Encoding Libraries

Example:

- he.js
- xss-filters

For non-React contexts.

---

### ✅ 15. Monitor CSP Violations

Enterprise apps use:

- Sentry
- Datadog
- Custom endpoints

CSP violations reveal:

- New attack attempts
- Third-party issues
- Compromised dependencies

---

## Real-World XSS Attacks

- Twitter XSS worm (2010)
- MySpace Samy worm (5th largest security incident ever)
- eBay stored XSS
- British Airways XSS attack (Magecart)

Millions of dollars lost.

---

## Modern Enterprise Prevention Strategy

1. React with escaping
2. CSP with nonces
3. Trusted Types
4. Server-side sanitization
5. Input validation
6. Monitoring
7. Continuous dependency audits (Snyk, npm audit)

Used by:

- Amazon
- Google
- Facebook
- Vercel
- Slack
- LinkedIn

---

# 4. Combining All Best Practices

Modern enterprise apps use a **security layered architecture**:

```txt
Frontend Escaping
     ↓
Sanitization
     ↓
CSP + Trusted Types
     ↓
Secure Cookies + SameSite
     ↓
OAuth 2.1 + Passkeys
     ↓
Session Rotation
     ↓
Server Validation
     ↓
WAF + Rate Limiting
     ↓
Monitoring + Alerts
```

This ensures:

- XSS prevention
- CSRF protection
- Session security
- Auth safety
- Scaling
- Compliance

---

# 5. Senior Engineer Interview Answer

> Modern session management combines short-lived access tokens, rotating refresh tokens stored in HttpOnly and Secure cookies, session ID rotation, activity-based inactivity timeouts, and multi-device session controls to ensure secure and scalable authentication. CSP is one of the strongest browser-side defenses, with strict directives like `script-src 'self' 'nonce-xxx'`, `frame-ancestors 'none'`, `object-src 'none'`, and enforced HTTPS ensuring XSS, clickjacking, and injection resistance. XSS prevention requires multiple layers — escaping user input using frameworks like React, avoiding `innerHTML`, sanitizing rich text with DOMPurify, enforcing strong CSP, adopting Trusted Types, and validating input server-side. Combining these techniques, enterprise-grade apps like Google, LinkedIn, Vercel, and Slack build resilient, secure systems for billions of users worldwide.
